name: Methamphetamine Addiction
overview: Methamphetamine is a powerful stimulant that causes extreme dopamine release, strong psychological dependence, and significant medical and psychiatric harm. Kentucky has seen a sustained rise in meth-related hospitalizations and deaths — often complicated by co-use with opioids. There are no FDA-approved medications for meth addiction, which makes structured behavioral treatment and integrated mental health care the standard of care.
signs: Dental decay ("meth mouth") and skin sores
Long waking periods (days) followed by extended sleep
Hyperactivity, rapid speech, hyperfocus on repetitive tasks
Deteriorating work, school, and family functioning
Using despite escalating medical, legal, or psychiatric consequences

treatmentApproach.title: Medically monitored stabilization
treatmentApproach.detail: While meth withdrawal is not typically life-threatening, medical monitoring supports safety in patients with psychiatric symptoms, cardiac complications, or polysubstance use.

treatmentApproach.title: Residential treatment
treatmentApproach.detail: Structured residential care gives patients protected time during the acute post-use psychiatric window and supports early skill-building.

treatmentApproach.title: Contingency management
treatmentApproach.detail: Contingency management (CM) is the most effective behavioral treatment for stimulant use disorder and is supported by strong evidence.

treatmentApproach.title: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy
treatmentApproach.detail: CBT addresses relapse triggers, thought patterns, and skill deficits that drive return-to-use.

treatmentApproach.title: Integrated mental health care
treatmentApproach.detail: Meth use frequently produces or worsens anxiety, depression, and psychotic symptoms; integrated psychiatric and SUD care is essential.

localContext: Kentucky continues to see rising methamphetamine-related deaths, with meth increasingly involved in polysubstance overdoses alongside fentanyl. Louisville's demand for stimulant-focused treatment has grown meaningfully over the past five years.
medicaidCoverageSummary: All six Kentucky Medicaid MCOs cover detox, residential, PHP, IOP, and outpatient SUD services for stimulant use disorder. There are no FDA-approved medications specifically for meth addiction, but behavioral treatment and integrated psychiatric care for co-occurring conditions are covered.

faqs.a: Meth withdrawal is usually not life-threatening in the way alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal can be, but it produces severe depression and suicidal ideation in some patients. Medical and psychiatric monitoring is strongly recommended.

faqs.q: Is there a medication for meth addiction?
faqs.a: No medication is FDA-approved for stimulant use disorder. Evidence-based behavioral treatments — particularly contingency management and CBT — are the standard of care, along with treatment of any co-occurring psychiatric conditions.

faqs.q: Does Kentucky Medicaid cover meth rehab?
faqs.a: Yes. All Kentucky Medicaid MCOs cover detox, residential, PHP, IOP, and outpatient services for stimulant use disorder based on medical-necessity determinations.
